Now let's talk about the DCO Infinity Strategy. This is how I like to set up my warm audiences. It's not the only way. But the cool thing about this is when you combine CBO, Campaign Budget Optimization, with DCO, which is Dynamic Content Optimization, it just becomes a completely hands-off experience. And let me just show you what I mean by that. So this is an account we are in the process of building up. So we're about to set up this campaign setup for this account. And this is kind of how we do it. One thing that I get asked is, what kind of audiences should I go after in my warm, in my hot, what kind of time periods, et cetera? Here's an example of a fairly new account. So warm audiences, if you go back to the full funnel system, our people, for example, that have viewed a video, engaged on Facebook or Instagram, fans, people that may be on your email list, generally people that have not clicked. So cold audiences for me are not engaged and not clicked. Warm audiences are not clicked, but they have engaged. And then hot audiences are the ones that have clicked into my site. That's the way I like to split it up. So my warm audiences are people that know of the brand or the product already, but have not decided to click. So for example, here for this particular account, we've got only two audiences to play with. So we've been running video view campaigns top of funnel. We've been running website conversion campaigns. So we have video retargeting data for one or two videos that we're running. We've also been running ads and racking up interest on our page, people viewing our page, clicking our likes are starting to pick up, but we don't really have enough fans to retarget Facebook fans, for example. So we've just gone for the two audiences. The next question, so once we've kind of figured out which audiences we're going for, the next question is then, you know, should I target people within seven days, 14, 30, XYZ? Our default position is to start with 30 days. The reason is I want a big enough audience size. So this account right now is spending about $500 a day. So it's not a huge amount. So these audiences are not picking up a huge amount of data, video views, sure, but not the engages, but I want a lot more data. So I want to expand the time span to 30 days before I look to trim it down. Because here's the thing, if someone's viewed a video or they've engaged on my page but not clicked, over time they'll forget who the brand is and the product is, and therefore the ads become less and less effective. If and when I go to scale this up, I have a few different options. Maybe I trim it down and create a seven day view audience, a 14 day, 21, 28, or maybe I just leave it at 30 days. And that is driven by the results that I see. So when I start off a new campaign, new ad account, et cetera, I go through the grad testing, go through prospecting, set up my retargeting, which is my hot campaign. When I come to my warm, I always start with 30 days and then let the data tell me what to do next. So that's the first part of set up. So here, for example, I've gone for all genders, all age ranges, again, you could split that going forward. And you look at that through your breakdown reports to see how performance is looking. Now the other thing then I'd like to do is to use DCO. DCO allows me to create one ad with multiple variants of my ad. And you can see in the breakdown report what those variants are. So I can't actually see here because I don't have any data. But when you click on breakdown, you'll see a breakdown for your dynamic creative to see all the variants in play. This hasn't launched yet, so I can't see it. And we'll show you what that looks like in a separate video. But here, what I like to do, and this is my specific config, is I like to create multiple variants of my body copy, multiple image and kind of image formats. And then I can create one video and then different headlines. They're the three things that I like to test. Generally what I would do is try and pick three different cop ad copies, two different formats, so video versus image, and then a few different headlines and put those into the mix. I will then test that over time and we'll show you as a separate video of kind of what that then looks like to look at the best performance that I can maintain within this ad set, within the campaign over a period of time. Because the whole goal of creating the infinity strategy for my warm and hot audiences is that it's set once and then forget. To the extent that, one thing to recognize is this, when someone enters your further down the funnel, when they enter an audience, so people that have engaged in a video over the last 30 days, that therefore is an audience that refreshes every 30 days. So what I wanna do is set that time period just about right, with the right ads, that I don't need to worry about it, because the audience is set. Like once you've got a good audience here, it's constantly refilling. As long as you're doing a good job in your top of funnel, in your cold ads, then whatever trickled through into your warm, it's refreshing. This is refreshing for me every 30 days. Therefore, the ads don't generally fatigue and especially when you're running DCO, this Facebook is cycling through the best ad for the best user at the best time. And what this actually gives you is, and this is why I like to run different formats, is for example, when you have a DCO and you have an image ad, you have a video ad, Facebook knows based on that user, which format to serve based on how they react. So if for example, I'm someone who never watches videos on Facebook in terms of my newsfeed or my ads, Facebook's gonna serve me a static image because it knows generally, a video's a waste of time on me. And the vice versa is true. Also, it might be the case that I never look at video on my mobile, but I do on my desktop. Again, Facebook will know that data, so that they're gonna serve an ad to me at a specific time and a specific device. They will do that and they will do that better than I can because at the time the ad goes into the auction, Facebook will pick the right variant out of your DCO. So that's why when you create your warm campaign, you're able to push the right, like the right ad format, the right copy, the right headline to the user because Facebook's learning that. So as the data starts to mature, you will get to a stage where you don't really need to touch anything. So your ad sets are running, you've got your time period right, your other data points which you test and analyze, but your ads generally don't change. Now the other thing that I would also point out is the way I like to run my DCOs, because at the moment you can only run one DCO per ad set, there's something Facebook are trying to allow you to do more, but because there's only one DCO per ad set, I like to create one DCO for one angle. So here you can see I've got two testimonial angles and I've got one user generated content angle, which for this is someone installing the device, sharing a video, talking about their experience, for example, and the testimonial ads are kind of written testimonials that we use images and videos for. So the different angles that we've got, so this testimonial angle is different, testimonial one and testimonial two, two are different angles pulled in for that ad. And what we're doing here is for every angle, we're testing it against each of the different audiences and we're running it and within that DCO is just that angle. So the copy, the creative, everything is just focused on that one angle and this is one of the other ways that we're able to maintain consistency because you can have two angles, you can have five angles and if you've got five angles here, that means that these two audiences, which are video viewers and FBIG engages, would be duplicate five times, but I don't need to run them all at the same time. I can pause and start ad sets here as I need to and I'll show you in another video how I've used that in a different scenario. And again, that means especially when you're starting to spend more and more top of funnel and you need to cycle more angles in and sometimes different audiences react with some angles better than others, you can just pause and restart, pause and start different audiences, but you don't touch the ads. Like the ads are just written, you know they've done your research, you understand your avatar, you understand which angles have worked over time. So the ads, you don't need to change, but what you do is change which angles that they're seeing based on performance. So this is how I like to structure my warm and hot audiences as well, to give Facebook everything it needs so that I can actually step back. So the way I operate right now is my warm and hot audiences and further down that funnel as well, I don't actually optimize monitor or edit that frequently because I do the work upfront, I set up the right audiences and the ads, I do my research and run it into a test phase in my warm audience. So one of the other questions that also comes up is where do I do my testing for my warm audiences? Do I do a separate graduation testing process? Do I have separate campaigns? I don't do that. And there's two reasons for that. Number one is the audience size is too small. So when you look at doing testing with your cold audiences, and one of the questions that comes up is how do I know that my testing audience and ads are not competing with my prospecting? Generally, you're talking about fairly big audiences, even 100,000, 500,000 a million, because of the way I do grad testing, there's a really low chance it's gonna be a crossover. If you're doing testing with your warm audiences, generally, and this is just a generalization, most people will have smaller audiences. So when you are doing your graduation testing on warm and you have a prospecting campaign on warm, chances are they are competing. So for that reason, I don't do grad testing on warm. It's not to say you can't, especially if you've got bigger audiences, absolutely. And in that case, you'd set up the same process, phase one for audience, phase two for ads. But I do my testing here, and this is how I like to do my testing. So for example, if I'm about to launch this campaign, I don't know which of these, if any, are gonna work, but I push them into DCO with their variables, with their different ad copy creatives, et cetera. I'll run some spend against them, and then I'll go to my breakdown report. I'll look at my overall RIS and overall CPA, and then I'll look at my breakdown to see which variants are working, and then I'll take out the ones that are not working. Now, if I run out of variants, I might make a decision as to whether I create a new DCO. I would prefer to create a new one rather than duplicate because it's picked up some bad history and some bad data, and then decide what to do. And over a period of one to two weeks, as that data starts to fill in, I will have reduced it down. So it could be, for example, that UGC works great, and only Testimonial 1 works, or maybe Testimonial 1 works here, but not on here. I'll continue running those, and that's fine. I'll let the data dictate. So initially, I brought in my avatar-based information and my ideas for content, but then I now find that the data is telling me something else, and then I continue to run it. Once I've got good performance, then I let it run. I may review it every two to four weeks just to check in if there's new ideas. Like, for example, maybe we've got new user-generated content piece that's come through from a user, a customer, for example, they may have sent us an amazing video of them using the product, and now I wanna test that. But I might test it in my code and my existing customers first before I bring it into Warmore. Maybe I bring it here first and I start testing. But then I introduce that as a new ad set. So for example, if I've got new user-generated content ad, I'd create a new ad set, duplicate it with the same audience, et cetera, with a new DCO and start testing. And the way you also need to bear in mind on running ad sets and ads is it comes down to budget. If you have a, you know, if you're starting off early on, like, I can't remember what we set as the budget here, but it'll be a fairly small budget. So $70 a day is the start budget. It's not a huge amount, especially when you consider we have six ad sets. So that's just over $10 per ad set per day if it was evenly spread out. And then you've got your variance of your ads between that. So $70 is not gonna go far. So what I would actually do is only launch four ad sets at most, maybe even less, and then give that time to pick up data and then make decisions off of that. Maybe those three ad sets work fine, straight off the bat, maybe they don't. But this is the kind of process and kind of engineering to build in that stability. Because when I, you know, when I talk about stability and optimization, I start by optimizing and optimizing my cold funnel, but I wanna get to a stage where my warm and hot, where it becomes completely hands off, that I don't need to check it too often. It's a well, well churned, it's a great machine. I've got it all working, the stats looking great. And then I focus all my attention on grant testing and prospecting at the top of the funnel, because that's where the money's made. Like don't be fooled by thinking because your ROAS is good and warm, your ROAS is good and hot, but that's where the money's being made. That's where it's being closed. That's the end of the conveyor belt, but that's not where it's made. Where it's made is top of funnel. I wanna start increasing my spend there, but I can only do that when the rest of the funnel is operating. So that's one of the biggest things to take away from that full funnel system is I'm building each step in by looking at and optimizing the metrics. And in my warm campaigns, I want to build it up to a stage where it's completely hands off.
